content.text: Irrational numbers have infinite continued fraction expansions.

content.title: Infinite Continued Fraction
content.content:
An **infinite continued fraction** $[a_0; a_1, a_2, \ldots]$ 
represents $\lim_{n \to \infty} c_n$ where $c_n = [a_0; a_1, \ldots, a_n]$.

content.title: Convergence
content.content:
Every infinite simple continued fraction converges to an irrational number. 
Conversely, every irrational number has a unique infinite simple CF expansion.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Approximation Quality
content.content:
If $c_k = p_k/q_k$ is a convergent of $\alpha$, then:
\[
\left| \alpha - \frac{p_k}{q_k} \right| < \frac{1}{q_k q_{k+1}} < \frac{1}{q_k^2}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text: This means convergents are *very good* rational approximations.

content.title: Quadratic Irrational
content.content:
A **quadratic irrational** is a number of the form $\frac{a + \sqrt{d}}{c}$ 
where $a, c, d \in \mathbb{Z}$, $c \neq 0$, and $d > 0$ is not a perfect square.

content.title: Lagrange's Theorem
content.content:
A real number has an eventually periodic continued fraction expansion 
if and only if it is a quadratic irrational.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: The Golden Ratio
content.problem:
$\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} = [1; \overline{1}] = [1; 1, 1, 1, \ldots]$.

The convergents are ratios of consecutive Fibonacci numbers: 
$1, 2, \frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{3}, \frac{8}{5}, \ldots$

content.content:
The CF of $\alpha$ is purely periodic (i.e., $\alpha = [\overline{a_0; a_1, \ldots, a_n}]$) 
if and only if $\alpha > 1$ and $-1 < \alpha' < 0$, where $\alpha'$ is 
the algebraic conjugate.
content.label: Theorem
